dependencies.notes: orders-db is a stateful Postgres cluster. EBS volume is the underlying storage — if EBS has problems, so does the DB. S3 is used for WAL archive. If S3 is unreachable, WAL archiving will fail silently and the pg_wal directory on disk will grow until disk is full. This happened in INC-4021. Check S3 connectivity as part of any disk issue investigation.

common_issues.title: Disk quota exceeded — writes failing
symptoms: orders-service returning 500 on all write operations
Postgres logs: 'could not write to file... No space left on device'
likely_causes: WAL archive cron not running — WAL files accumulate in pg_wal indefinitely
S3 bucket for WAL archive unreachable — archive command failing silently
Unusual write volume (e.g., during incident with high retry rates)
Table bloat from dead tuples not being VACUUMed

steps.action: Confirm disk is full and identify largest directories
steps.note: Check which directory is largest. Usually it's pg_wal or the main data directory. pg_wal being unexpectedly large = WAL archive problem.

steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT archived_count, failed_count, last_archived_wal, last_failed_wal, last_failed_time FROM pg_stat_archiver;"
steps.note: failed_count > 0 and last_failed_time recent = archive is failing. last_archived_wal being very old = archive hasn't run in a while. Either way, WAL is accumulating on disk.

steps.action: Check if archive cron is running
steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- crontab -l && grep -i wal /var/log/syslog | tail -20
steps.note: The WAL archive cron should run every hour. If there's no crontab entry, it may have been wiped during a pod restart. This was the root cause of INC-4021.

steps.action: Prune WAL archive to free space — DO THIS CAREFULLY
steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- pg_archivecleanup /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_wal/ $(psql -U postgres -t -c "SELECT pg_walfile_name(pg_current_wal_lsn());")
steps.note: pg_archivecleanup is safe to run. It only removes WAL files that are no longer needed for recovery. Do NOT manually rm files from pg_wal — you can corrupt the cluster. Get a second pair of eyes before running this.

steps.action: Extend EBS volume if pruning isn't enough
steps.note: You can extend EBS volumes online without downtime. After extending, you need to resize the filesystem too: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- resize2fs /dev/xvda. Volume ID is in the PVC — kubectl get pvc orders-db-pvc -o json | jq '.spec.volumeName'

steps.action: Restart Postgres after recovery to clear write errors
steps.note: Postgres may be in a bad state after a disk-full event. Restart forces clean connection pool and clears cached errors. Monitor logs after restart.

common_issues.escalation: Disk full on orders-db is P1 — order writes are failing. Page commerce on-call immediately. This is time-sensitive.

common_issues.title: Slow queries / high DB CPU
likely_causes: Sequential scan on orders table (40M+ rows — very expensive without index)
analytics dashboard firing 14 concurrent heavy queries (known issue)
VACUUM not running — table bloat causing planner to make bad choices
N+1 query pattern from orders-service (fetches order then fetches each item separately)

steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT substring(query, 1, 100) as query, calls, round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) as mean_ms, round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) as total_ms FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;"
steps.note: pg_stat_statements must be in shared_preload_libraries. If you get an error, it's not enabled. The fix requires a restart — coordinate with Dave.

steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) <paste_slow_query_here>;"
steps.note: Look for 'Seq Scan' on the orders table. If you see it, there's a missing index. 'Bitmap Heap Scan' is OK. 'Index Scan' is ideal.

steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_<name> ON orders(<columns>);"
steps.note: Always use CONCURRENTLY in production. This takes longer but doesn't lock the table. Monitor progress: SELECT phase, blocks_done, blocks_total FROM pg_stat_progress_create_index;

steps.action: If analytics dashboard is culprit, disable it
steps.note: There's a ticket to rewrite the analytics queries against a materialized view. JIRA COM-512. Not done yet. Disabling the dashboard is the only mitigation for now.

steps.action: Run VACUUM if table bloat is suspected
steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM ANALYZE orders;"
steps.note: VACUUM ANALYZE is safe to run concurrently with normal operations (not VACUUM FULL). It reclaims dead tuple space and updates statistics. Run during off-peak. Takes 10-30 minutes on a large table.

common_issues.escalation: If queries are causing customer-facing timeouts, escalate to P1. Otherwise P2 during business hours.

common_issues.title: Replication lag / replica falling behind
symptoms: Replica shows stale data compared to primary
likely_causes: High write volume on primary — replica can't apply WAL fast enough
Network congestion between primary and replica AZ
Long-running query on replica holding back apply process

steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT client_addr, state, sent_lsn, write_lsn, flush_lsn, replay_lsn, (sent_lsn - replay_lsn) AS replication_delay FROM pg_stat_replication;"
steps.note: replication_delay in bytes. Divide by ~1MB to get rough seconds of lag. If delay is 0, replica is caught up.

steps.action: Check if read traffic is being routed to lagging replica
steps.command: kubectl get configmap orders-db-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .read_replica_enabled'
steps.note: If read_replica_enabled is true and replica is lagging, reads may see stale data. Temporarily disable read routing: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service DB_READ_REPLICA_ENABLED=false

steps.action: Check for blocking queries on replica
steps.command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-replica-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT pid, query_start, state, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle' ORDER BY query_start ASC LIMIT 10;"
steps.note: Long-running queries on the replica can block WAL apply. Terminate if safe to do so.

common_issues.escalation: Replication lag > 5 minutes is P2. Lag > 30 minutes or replication stopped entirely is P1 — page DBA on-call.

general_health_checks: kubectl exec orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c 'SELECT 1' — should return immediately
kubectl exec orders-db-primary-0 -- df -h — disk usage should be < 75%
kubectl exec orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_replication;" — should show active replicas
Check Datadog: 'Orders DB' dashboard — connections, disk usage, replication lag, slow queries
kubectl exec orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT last_archived_wal, last_failed_time FROM pg_stat_archiver;" — last_failed_time should be null
known_gaps: WAL archive cron restoration after pod restart is manual — not yet automated (post-INC-4021 TODO)
No runbook for point-in-time recovery (PITR) — find the DBA team if you need this
pg_stat_statements not consistently enabled across all environments
Replica failover procedure not fully tested since cluster was provisioned — JIRA PLAT-301
EBS volume extension procedure is manual — not in Terraform
notes: orders-db is the source of truth for all order data. Be extremely careful with any direct SQL modifications to production. Always run SELECT before UPDATE/DELETE to verify what you're about to change. Get a second set of eyes on any destructive operation. There is no undo for a bad UPDATE — only point-in-time recovery, which takes hours.
